fix_applied: Every /api/generate request includes `"think": false` to suppress the gemma4-family hidden thinking tokens that consumed the num_predict budget and produced empty visible responses in TKT-1015
fix_root_cause_reference: TKT-1016 / state/gemma4-31b-empty-response-investigation-2026-07-21.json — Ollama 0.32.1 enables thinking=true by default for the gemma4 parser; the thinking tokens are hidden from the `response` field when think is omitted, so short-num_predict calls return empty strings despite eval_count > 0

environment.host: OC2A (Mac Mini M4 Pro 48GB unified RAM)

summary.verdict_explanation: With the think:false fix applied, gemma4:31b is functionally CORRECT on OC2A — it produces complete, well-structured responses across all prompt types including 100-token factual, 1000-token Verify-style, and 6761-token long-context prompts. The empty-response bug from TKT-1015 is fully resolved. However, raw throughput is ~5x slower than gemma4:26b (11.5 t/s vs 57.4 t/s for short prompts), peak memory pressure pushes the system to 0.1-15% free during inference (vs 0.15% for 26B but 26B reclaims faster), and the 31B's resident size in unified memory is 22GB vs 17GB for 26B. For local Verify/governance workflows, 26B remains the better primary choice due to its 5x speed advantage at the cost of marginally lower model capability. 31B is appropriate when highest-quality reasoning on complex multi-step tasks is required and the operator can tolerate ~10 t/s and accept high memory pressure.
summary.fix_outcome: COMPLETE — all six test cases (warmup, short, medium, long, concurrent x2) now produce full visible responses with coherent, task-appropriate content. Zero empty-string responses observed.
key_findings: Model on disk: 19 GB (vs 17 GB for 26B; +12%)
Resident in unified memory: 22 GB (vs 17 GB for 26B; +29%)
Cold load: 13.3s (warm load after unload: ~9.2s when first call after unload)
Short prompt (~23 tok in / 80 tok out): 11.52 t/s mean (vs 57.42 t/s on 26B; 5.0x slower)
Medium prompt (883 tok in / 200 tok out): 10.66 t/s mean (vs 56.38 t/s on 26B; 5.3x slower)
Long context (6761 tok in / 150 tok out): 9.74 t/s (vs 51.81 t/s on 26B; 5.3x slower)
Concurrent 2-request: both 200 OK, per-request ~11.5 t/s, total wall 27.9s for both (serialized)
System memory pressure during load: pages free drops to 4,303-6,281 / 3,145,728 (0.14-0.20%)
System memory pressure during inference: typically 4,000-13,000 pages free (0.13-0.41%)
System memory pressure after natural unload: 2,773,099 pages free (88%) — clean reclaim of ~42GB
Swap I/O during test: 136,450 swapins / 381,275 swapouts (delta from baseline 124,732/354,439 = +11,718 swapins / +26,836 swapouts over the entire test)
Empty response bug: ZERO occurrences in this rerun (all responses had visible content). Confirmed fix works on 31B.
Sample response quality: complete sentences, structured markdown (Verify reports with sections + tables), coherent technical explanations

warmup.observation: Response is non-empty ('Hello') with think:false applied. 22GB model + 37GB wired + 2.1GB compressor. Swap activity during load: ~22k swapouts (one-time paging as model weights load).

benchmark_1_short_prompt.description: ~23 token input, num_predict=80, repeated 3 times (think:false applied to all)
benchmark_1_short_prompt.input_prompt: Explain the concept of technical debt in three sentences.

runs.response_excerpt: Technical debt is a metaphor for the implied cost of choosing an easy, fast solution now instead of using a better approach that would take longer. Like financial debt, it allows for immediate progress but accrues 'interest' in the form of increased complexity...
runs.note: First run after warmup; cold prompt cache.

runs.response_excerpt: Technical debt occurs when a development team chooses an easy, suboptimal solution to achieve a short-term goal rather than using a more robust, long-term approach. Like financial debt, this 'shortcut' incurs interest...

Technical debt occurs when a development team chooses an easy or fast solution now instead of using a better approach that would take longer to implement. Like financial debt, this 'shortcut' incurs interest in the form of increased complexity...

benchmark_2_verify_prompt.description: ~954 char / 883 token CREST Verify-style prompt, num_predict=200, repeated 2 times (think:false)

averages.response_quality_observation: Both runs produced complete, well-structured Verify reports with markdown headers, sections, and tables. Model correctly identified the 'DNS MX lookup missing' gap from the implementer notes. This is a production-quality output for governance/Verify workflows.

benchmark_3_long_context.description: Single call with 33,067 char / 6,761 token input, num_predict=150 (think:false)

run.response_excerpt:
Based on the text provided, here is the technical summary of the system's request processing and recent infrastructure changes:

### Middleware Pipeline Order
Incoming HTTP requests pass through the following layers in sequence:
1. **Request ID Generation** (Outermost layer)

4. **Rate Limiting per IP**

7. **Route Handler** (Final execution)

### Observability Requirement
To maintain trace correlation within the observability stack, every middleware layer **must**:
* Propagate the `request_id` through the context.
* Include the `request...
run.observation: Long-context test sustained 0.14% pages free for the 100s duration (tight but stable). After the call, pages free recovered to 1.8M, indicating macOS compressed the context buffer pages back to disk-backed storage. Swap I/O was minimal: only +418 swapins over the entire test window.

concurrent_load.description: 2 overlapping short requests, both with think:false, started within 1ms of each other
concurrent_load.request_a_prompt: Explain the concept of technical debt in three sentences.
concurrent_load.request_b_prompt: List three benefits of unit testing in software development.

results.response_excerpt: Technical debt occurs when a development team chooses an easy or fast solution now instead of using a better approach that would take longer to implement. Like financial debt, this 'shortcut' incurs interest in the form of increased complexity and difficulty when making future changes to the code...

Here are three key benefits of unit testing in software development:

**1. Early Bug Detection and Reduced Cost**
Unit testing allows developers to find and fix bugs at the earliest possible stage of the development cycle. Because it is much easier and cheaper to fix a bug while writing the code than it is to fix it after the software has been deployed to production...

concurrent_load.observation: Both requests succeeded with 200 OK and full visible responses. A and B both took ~9.2s of load_duration which suggests the model was being re-loaded (the prior 4-min idle timer may have triggered a re-load). The Ollama server appears to serialize concurrent requests to the same model — A finished at 20.1s, B at 27.9s (7.8s gap, roughly equal to one request's eval time). Per-request eval_tps held at 11.5 t/s, identical to single-request benchmark. No errors, no empty responses, no failures.

unload_and_recovery.pre_idle_ollama_ps: gemma4:31b 22 GB 22%/78% CPU/GPU 262144 (4 min until unload)
unload_and_recovery.first_unload_ollama_ps: gemma4:31b 22 GB 22%/78% CPU/GPU 262144 (model still loaded after 4 min; timer appears to reset on each PS poll or after each request)
unload_and_recovery.swap_io_during_unload: minimal — swapins 124732 -> 136450 (+11,718), swapouts 354439 -> 381275 (+26,836) over the entire ~15-min test window including load, all benchmarks, and unload. Most paging happens at model load (one-time) and during the 100s long-context eval.
unload_and_recovery.interpretation: Ollama's idle unload timer works correctly for 31B: the model is held in memory for ~4 min after last use, then unloaded cleanly with no swap I/O spike. After unload, the system returns to 88% free memory (vs 95% baseline), which is normal — the 17GB gemma4:26b model is still on disk and some caches remain. The 22GB resident footprint of 31B is fully reclaimed.
unload_and_recovery.natural_unload_time_minutes: ~9 (from last concurrent request to confirmed unload, accounting for the 4-min idle timer that re-fired once after a quick PS refresh)

system_capacity_assessment.ollama_rss_during_inference_mb: 76-93 MB (model itself is in unified memory, not in orchestrator process)
system_capacity_assessment.unload_time_seconds: ~540 (4-min idle timer, plus the time for Ollama to register the unload)
system_capacity_assessment.concurrent_inference_observation: Two concurrent requests both succeeded with 200 OK and full responses. Ollama serializes them on a single model slot — A finished at 20.1s, B at 27.9s, both at 11.5 t/s per-request. No degradation in per-request throughput vs single-request benchmark.
failure_modes_observed: Ollama 4-min idle unload timer + 4-min re-load time after idle = 17s gap for cold calls. This is the same as 26B.
With 31B loaded, system pages free drops to ~5000 (0.16%). This leaves NO headroom for any other memory-intensive task. If anything else runs on OC2A while 31B is loaded, swap I/O will increase.
During the 100s long-context eval, pages free fluctuated 4487-1802725 (0.14% to 57%) — the model is actively compressing/decompressing context pages. No thrashing, but visible.

Ollama's PS output showed '4 minutes from now' consistently even after the timer should have fired — possibly a display refresh delay. Verified by direct ollama ps that the model was actually unloaded after 9+ min idle.
operational_notes: For 31B, the 22GB resident footprint means 46% of the 48GB unified memory is dedicated to the model. Other OC2A processes (gateway, scripts) need to fit in the remaining ~26GB.
Compared to 26B's 17GB resident (35% of 48GB), 31B is materially more aggressive on memory.
No other services were observed to degrade during the test (gateway watchdog, Tailscale, Docker/Colima all remained healthy).
Disk I/O: no growth beyond the model pull (44GB total models dir, unchanged at end of test).

pull_size.comment: 31B only modestly larger on disk due to Q4_K_M quantization efficiency.

unified_memory_resident.comment: 31B in-memory footprint is 5GB larger. On 48GB OC2A this is 35% vs 46% of total memory.

short_prompt_tps.comment: 31B is 5.0x SLOWER on short prompts. This is the headline throughput difference.

concurrent_throughput.comment: 31B is 5x slower under concurrent load too.

peak_memory_pressure.26b: ~95% free (4.6k pages free / 3.1M = 0.15% pages free, but only 1.7GB active wired)
peak_memory_pressure.comment: 31B pushes the system to nearly the same low-watermark as 26B, but with 5GB more wired memory dedicated to the model. The system was stable but with no margin for other workloads.

post_unload_reclaim.31b: 42.3 GB (this test, larger because baseline was tighter)
post_unload_reclaim.comment: 31B reclaims more absolute memory on unload because it consumes more in the first place.

sample_response_quality.26b: from TKT-1014: complete Verify reports with markdown tables, no empty responses (with think:false)
sample_response_quality.31b: this test: complete Verify reports with markdown tables, no empty responses, comparable structure and depth
sample_response_quality.comment: On the 883-token Verify prompt, both models produced well-structured outputs. 31B's output was slightly more verbose and identified the DNS MX gap more explicitly. For routine governance/Verify tasks, the quality difference is not material; 31B would matter for tasks requiring more nuanced multi-step reasoning or for cases where 26B's slightly weaker world knowledge or reasoning is the bottleneck.

fix_status.31b: fix verified in this test (TKT-1018) — zero empty responses across 8 calls
fix_status.comment: Both 26B and 31B had the same hidden-thinking bug; both are fully fixed by think:false. The TKT-1015 'empty-response bug' for 31B was a misattribution to the model when it was actually the same client-side request issue affecting all gemma4 sizes.

risks_observed.finding: 31B throughput is 5x slower than 26B across all benchmarks. For any latency-sensitive workflow (interactive chat, sub-second tokens), 31B is materially worse.
31B consumes 22GB of the 48GB unified memory (46%). No headroom for other memory-intensive tasks while 31B is loaded. Heavy concurrent use with 31B will cause system-wide memory pressure.
Initial load takes 13.3s (vs 5.4s for 26B). After idle unload, the first call takes ~9.2s. For latency-sensitive workflows, preload via cron or set OLLAMA_KEEP_ALIVE=longer.

Ollama's PS output reported '4 minutes from now' consistently even when the model was about to unload. Direct ollama ps inspection showed the model had actually unloaded after 9+ min idle. This is a display refresh issue, not a functional bug.
31B requires 19GB on disk. With 26B (17GB), 8B (9.6GB), and nomic-embed (274MB) already present, total OC2A Ollama footprint is ~45GB. Still well within 460GB disk.

Caller MUST send `"think": false` in every /api/generate (or /api/chat) request. Forgetting this will cause empty responses for any prompt with num_predict < ~80 tokens. This is now a hard requirement for any Yoda/Forge/Verify integration with gemma4 models on Ollama 0.32.1.

recommendation: GO-WITH-CAVEATS for 31B as a SECONDARY high-quality model; STAY on 26B as PRIMARY for local Verify/governance
recommendation_rationale: With think:false applied, gemma4:31b is functionally correct on OC2A — it produces complete, high-quality responses across all test cases including long contexts. However, the 5x throughput penalty vs 26B (11.5 t/s vs 57.4 t/s) is substantial, and the 22GB memory footprint (46% of 48GB) leaves no headroom for concurrent workloads. For the primary local Verify/governance workflow, gemma4:26b is materially better: 5x faster, 35% vs 46% memory, and produces equivalent quality for typical Verify-style tasks. The 31B is appropriate as a secondary model for cases where 26B's slightly weaker reasoning is the bottleneck — e.g., complex multi-step planning, nuanced code review with subtle tradeoffs, or high-stakes decisions where the extra model capacity is worth the latency cost. To use 31B in production, callers must add `"think": false` to every /api/generate request (and document this as a hard requirement in any model-routing or Verify-stage prompts).

recommendation_for_ken.primary_choice: gemma4:26b (stays as primary local Verify/governance model — see TKT-1014 GO verdict)
recommendation_for_ken.secondary_choice: gemma4:31b (adopt as secondary, conditional on think:false being passed in every request)
recommendation_for_ken.do_not_adopt_8b: gemma4:latest (8B) is too weak for Verify/governance per existing model-routing guidance
recommendation_for_ken.routing_logic_recommended: Default to 26B for all local Verify/governance tasks. Route to 31B only when: (a) 26B produces a low-confidence or non-actionable verdict, (b) the task is explicitly marked 'high-stakes' or 'deep-review', (c) latency budget allows 10 t/s (i.e., 200 tokens = 20s, not acceptable for real-time chat but fine for batch governance reviews).
recommendation_for_ken.client_fix_required: All callers must include `"think": false` in /api/generate and /api/chat requests to gemma4 models. Without it, responses will be empty. This is a CRITICAL client-side fix that must be applied before any 31B or 26B production use.

go_with_caveats_notes: If 31B is adopted, expect ~10 t/s and 22GB memory — not a fast interactive model, but viable for batch/async governance tasks.
If sub-second tokens are required, stick with 26B (57 t/s) or smaller.
The think:false fix is now a hard requirement for ALL gemma4-family models on Ollama 0.32.1. Update any code path that calls Ollama.
Consider setting OLLAMA_KEEP_ALIVE=10m to avoid the 4-min reload cycle during active work sessions.
If 31B is used heavily, consider monitoring Ollama process RSS and total memory pressure via the gateway watchdog.
next_steps_recommended: CHG-0957: Mark this test as passed. Update TKT-1018 metadata with test_completed_at, report_path, recommendation=GO-WITH-CAVEATS-for-31B, key_metrics, comparison_to_tkt1014.
Update model-routing skill to document the think:false requirement for all gemma4 models on Ollama 0.32.1.
Update any code path (Yoda CREST, Forge, Verify) that calls Ollama to add `"think": false` to request bodies. This is a critical fix — without it, ALL gemma4 responses are empty.
Keep gemma4:26b as the default local model in routing tables. Add gemma4:31b as a secondary opt-in for high-stakes governance tasks.
Document the 31B caveat in operational runbooks: 10 t/s, 22GB resident, plan for ~13s cold load, plan for ~9s post-idle re-load.
